diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Loin-to-groin radiation is the classic distribution of ureteric colic as the stone descends.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: ruptured or leaking abdominal aortic aneurysm
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: AAA can present with identical loin/flank pain — cannot be excluded on history alone.

symptoms.symptom_name: Extreme physical restlessness (pacing/writhing, cannot lie still)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Visceral colic pain is non-positional; restlessness distinguishes it from peritonitis where patients lie still.
A peritonitic patient characteristically lies perfectly still — constant movement argues against peritonism.

symptoms.symptom_name: Intractable vomiting, unable to tolerate oral intake

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Reflex autonomic vomiting is common in severe colic; here it also drives the need for parenteral (not oral) treatment and rehydration.
Stone-related mucosal trauma causes haematuria; patient-reportable so admissible in telehealth, but unverified.
Absence of fever/rigors/dysuria makes an actively infected kidney less likely at this moment, but does not exclude later infective obstruction.
Pain-driven sympathetic surge — a sealed clinician measurement; the AI can only infer distress from the video, not a measured BP/HR.
Renal-angle tenderness localises the pathology to the affected kidney/ureter — requires hands-on percussion the AI cannot perform.

symptoms.symptom_name: Soft, non-tender abdomen with no pulsatile mass and no peritonism

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: ruptured or leaking abdominal aortic aneurysm
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of a pulsatile/expansile mass and shock lowers AAA probability — but this is a sealed exam finding, so the AI cannot rely on it and must still route for in-person exclusion.

symptoms.symptom_name: Point-of-care urinalysis: marked microscopic haematuria, negative nitrites and leukocytes

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Haematuria without infection markers fits an obstructing calculus rather than infection — a clinician-run dipstick the AI cannot obtain.

symptoms.symptom_name: Non-contrast CT KUB showing a hyperdense radiopaque calculus in the urinary tract

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: CT KUB is the confirmatory investigation for a urinary calculus and simultaneously excludes vascular catastrophe — never available in real time on telehealth.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Confirms and sizes/locates the calculus and excludes a leaking AAA and other catastrophes — the AI recommends the pathway; it does not perform or interpret the scan.
Documents haematuria and screens for infection (nitrites/leukocytes) to flag an obstructed-infected kidney.
Assesses for obstruction-related acute kidney injury, especially with intractable vomiting and dehydration.
